summary: Apple responded to a €5 million Dutch antitrust fine by creating an alternative payment framework for dating apps that DHH argues is even more punitive than the original system. The new scheme still charges 27% commission, demands monthly sales reports, grants Apple audit rights over developers' books, and threatens App Store removal for non-compliance. DHH argues this isn't just Apple giving Dutch regulators the middle finger—it's a roadmap for extending this tax regime globally to any business with an app. He envisions Apple potentially applying this coercive reporting-and-invoice model to all apps worldwide, extracting 27% of economic activity from companies like Facebook under threat of platform expulsion.
key_points: Apple's 'compliance' with the Dutch order still charges 27% commission while adding burdensome reporting requirements, audit rights, and threatening punishments
The framework reveals Apple doesn't need technical payment integration to extract its tax—coerced reporting and monthly invoices work just as well
The audit rights and App Store expulsion threats create a coercive regime that no rational developer would voluntarily adopt
This Dutch-specific response could serve as a template for Apple to extend its commission model globally to all app-dependent businesses
The potential scope is staggering—if applied broadly, Apple could invoice companies like Facebook for 27% of all iPhone-derived economic activity
money_quotes: Apple has revealed an even more draconian, invasive, and frankly, terrifying scheme to collect their App Store tax from dating-app developers operating in The Netherlands.
By granting themselves audit rights 'pursuant to the entitlement's terms and conditions', they've shown how they can apply their 27% tax to any developer's economic activity at will.
Under the threat of App Store expulsion – with the subsequent ruin of any platform-dependent business – they don't even need a technical solution to collection.
Coerced reporting requirements together with monthly invoices might be old school, but it'll work just fine.
It's a small hop from there to amending the App Store guidelines globally such that this new tax regime will apply to all apps, from all businesses.
Apple has, perhaps inadvertently, just revealed to the world how they might go from a three-trillion dollar company to a hundred-trillion dollar company.
Just send every business dependent on an app a demand to audit their books, then mail them an invoice for 27% of their economic activity.
key_insight: Apple's malicious compliance with Dutch antitrust orders inadvertently reveals a scalable blueprint for taxing 27% of all app-dependent economic activity worldwide through coerced reporting and platform expulsion threats.
